# Questions in Livestream 261

Live Stream #261: Naughty Word Free Live Stream

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=626" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:26</a> | Would an ancient Egyptian man have been allowed to make offerings for a father who was a condemned traitor or serious criminal? | Yes. He said execution was the punishment in this life, but proper burial, mummification, and funerary offerings could still follow. | He said the Egyptians treated execution as the earthly penalty, not as a reason to deny private funerary rights. Burial, mummification, and offerings were privately paid, and he used Pentawer, involved in the assassination of Ramesses III, as an example of a serious traitor who could still receive proper burial. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=723" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:03</a> | Was any crime considered serious enough in Egypt to bring afterlife punishment? | He said explicit afterlife punishment appears late, especially in the Third Intermediate Period, and earlier problems were often treated as ritually mitigable. | He said most dangers could be mitigated by magic spells, and that afterlife punishment does not really appear in Egyptian religion until the Third Intermediate Period. He connected that development indirectly to Atenist influence, where divine punishment was imagined as taking place in this life. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=796" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:16</a> | Can God rewrite the past so that a real event never happened, or vice versa? | He said God could do so in principle, but saw no reason God would need to because providence already governs history without that step. | He answered that God has the power in principle, but that such a power would have no necessity because God already orders the present and knows every possible future. Since God is outside time and can arrange history as he wills from the start, he saw no reason that rewriting the past would ever need to be exercised. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=879" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:39</a> | What does he think is inside the large void in the Great Pyramid? | He said the safest guess is loose or low-density fill, not a hidden chamber, passage, or sensational discovery. | He said no one knows for certain, but his guess is loose stone, low-density stone, or gravel fill rather than a lost chamber or passageway. He noted that the void looks amorphous rather than architectural and compared the idea to known gravel fill in some pyramids. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=1266" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:06</a> | Which Christian emperor, king, or queen was closest to being the ideal Christian ruler? | He said it is almost impossible to answer because even good rulers have serious stains; bad rulers are easier to identify. | He said the only easy ideal answer would be Jesus, but that does not really answer the secular-ruler question. For earthly rulers, he argued that even good Christian rulers have public or private sins and historical stains, so identifying the best one is far harder than identifying obviously bad rulers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=1419" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:39</a> | How many semesters of biblical Hebrew and Koine Greek are needed to be ready for a PhD in Old Testament or New Testament? | He said Old Testament usually requires two years of Hebrew, New Testament three years of Greek, and some programs require both. | He framed the requirement by field: Old Testament doctoral work generally requires two years of Hebrew, while New Testament doctoral work generally requires three years of Greek. Depending on the school, a student may need both languages, so the practical answer can be two years of Hebrew plus three years of Greek. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=1913" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:53</a> | How did Pharaoh govern Egypt while traveling to perform religious duties? | He said Pharaoh delegated to viziers and a large bureaucracy; the vizier could manage state affairs while Pharaoh performed rituals. | He said Egypt had a massive bureaucracy that could keep the state running while Pharaoh was absent. The vizier, or sometimes two viziers, could act with delegated authority, use the seal ring, enact laws at Pharaoh's discretion, and manage affairs while Pharaoh traveled temple to temple for rituals. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=2005" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:25</a> | Was there anything only Pharaoh could do? | Yes. He said only Pharaoh could serve as high priest of every cult and appoint the crown prince. | He said Pharaoh alone could act as high priest of every Egyptian cult, a role the vizier was not permitted to fill. He also named succession as a royal-only act: a vizier could not designate the heir or appoint the crown prince. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=2105" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">35:05</a> | Were viziers allowed to start wars with other nations? | No. He said viziers could mount a civil defense, but they could not start wars or military campaigns. | He said the vizier was not in command of the military in a way that allowed him to launch foreign wars. A vizier could organize civil defense during an invasion and handle ordinary court business, law, and taxation disputes, but could not start campaigns on his own. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=2186" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">36:26</a> | Is division among Christians due to powers and principalities? | No. He said division is because of sin; not everything is demons or forces of darkness. | He rejected making demons or dark powers the explanation for every division. His answer was that much Christian division comes from human sin: people doing evil, disregarding others, and behaving badly toward one another without needing a demonic cause. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=2242" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:22</a> | Could a vizier judge criminals? | It depended on the case. He said viziers could judge lesser civil or financial matters, but capital cases belonged to the king. | He distinguished lesser cases from life-and-death cases. A vizier could adjudicate civil matters, financial disputes, and petty matters, but capital offenses were reserved for the king because cases involving death belonged to royal judgment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=2312" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:32</a> | Were there two viziers in ancient Egypt? | Sometimes. He said the answer depends on period, with one vizier in some eras and two in others, including the New Kingdom. | He said the number depends on the period: earlier periods such as the Middle Kingdom had one vizier, while the New Kingdom had two. He explained the change as an administrative response to growth, especially as the Delta became more heavily occupied and Egypt needed more regional administration. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=2401" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:01</a> | What does he think of the Faulk writing unit? | He explained it as 384 words, the daily count needed to write 100,000 words in ten months. | He said he developed the 384-word unit while thinking about a 100,000-word Liverpool PhD thesis and how much had to be written daily to finish in ten months. He treats it as a useful pacing tool because it is about a page and a half, easy to accumulate, and scalable for books or theses. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=2818" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">46:58</a> | Is repetition an underused or misunderstood writing technique? | He said it can be effective, especially in dialogue or tag phrases, but modern readers have low tolerance for overuse. | He said repetition once had more literary acceptance, but modern readers tolerate very little of it. It can still work when used sparingly, especially in dialogue, alliteration, or recurring verbal markers that identify a character, but he warned against making it a constant technique. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=3030" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:30</a> | Is it sinful to attend non-denominational churches? | No, as long as they are real Christian churches, though he warned that some non-denominational churches have serious problems. | He said attending a non-denominational church is not sinful if it is genuinely part of the universal Christian church. He added that some such churches have serious problems, just as some denominational churches do, so a person needs to know what they are actually joining. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=3211" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:31</a> | Is it sinful to start your own non-denominational church? | He said it depends on motive: starting one to preach Christ is not wrong, but ego or self-serving motives would change the assessment. | He said the motive controls the answer. Starting a church to spread the gospel is not wrong, but starting one to avoid accountability, taxes, discipline, or scrutiny over immoral conduct would be sinful. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=3585" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:45</a> | Could Merneptah's reference to Israel being laid waste refer to the plague in Numbers 25? | He said it is highly unlikely because the geography and chronology do not fit Merneptah's campaign. | He located the Numbers 25 plague east of the Jordan near the King's Road, beyond Moab, and said Merneptah's campaign does not appear to have gone there. He also noted a chronological problem: the plague would likely predate Merneptah's reign, while the Israel reference belongs to Merneptah's later campaign. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=3914" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:14</a> | How valuable would amber be in the time of Moses? | He said the value is hard to know because amber was not meaningfully present or traded in Egypt at that time. | He said amber was primarily associated with northern Europe and was not meaningfully present in Egypt as a local material or import. Because he could not find Egyptian amber jewelry or even clear evidence of an Egyptian term for it, he said its value in Egypt is hard to estimate. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=4143" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:09:03</a> | Is belief in corruption of human nature necessary for Christians? | He said inherited corruption is not mandatory, but federative responsibility and inherent corruption are hard to avoid. | He distinguished inherited corruption from federative responsibility and inherent corruption. He said Christians need not hold a specific inherited-corruption view of original sin, but the Old Testament bakes in corporate responsibility going back to Adam, and human beings plainly show an inherent propensity toward sin and rebellion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=4316" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:11:56</a> | Why did ancient Israelite military strategy favor slingers over archers? | He said stones and slings were cheap and easy to make, while bows and arrows required more specialized skill. | He said slings were accessible because stones were everywhere, leather slings were easy to make, and ordinary youths or shepherds could practice with them. Arrows, arrowheads, bows, and accurate archery required much more skill and training, so archers were not as universally available. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=5489" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:29</a> | Does Isaiah 13:17-19 contradict history about the Medes attacking Babylon? | He said the text first speaks of Babylonians rather than only the city, so the supposed contradiction needs the passage read carefully. | He read verses 17-18 as about the Babylonians generally, not specifically the city of Babylon, and said Opis can satisfy that attack language. Verse 19 compares Babylon's fall to Sodom and Gomorrah, which he took as overthrow rather than a wall siege, fitting a sudden takeover rather than a formal attack on the city. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=6304" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:04</a> | What are the seven seals, and why must someone worthy open them? | He said Revelation is cyclical, the seals mark a sealed scroll, and only one with authority, holiness, and worthiness can open it. | He explained Revelation as cycling through the same events from different angles. The seals are clay-seal style closures on a scroll, and opening them carries the responsibility of consummating the ordained course of history; only Christ, as the victorious lion and slain lamb with authority, holiness, power, and worthiness, can open it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=7018" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:56:58</a> | How prominent were Semites in the Delta after Avaris, including Dynasty 19? | He said there were Semites outside Avaris, especially Wadi Tumilat, but most left after Avaris; a smaller presence remained at Thebes. | He said there were significant Semitic populations beyond Avaris, especially in Wadi Tumilat because its pools supported sheep grazing, with some also south of the Pelusiac estuary and in western Thebes. After Avaris was abandoned, most Semites left, and the Theban group was the main remaining vestige he mentioned. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=7431" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:51</a> | Is icon veneration a moral hazard for idolatry in practice? | No. He distinguished veneration from worship and said he does not regard icon veneration itself as idolatry. | He defined icon veneration as asking a living saint for help, not worshiping an object in God's place or alongside God. He did not think it was efficacious or helpful, and warned that some Marian practices can go too far, but he did not classify ordinary veneration itself as idolatry. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=7847" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:47</a> | Why does he disagree that the plagues of Egypt were spiritual warfare? | He said that view overextends Exodus 12:12; the text does not make every plague a judgment against specific Egyptian gods. | He said the view rests on reading Exodus 12:12, about judgment against Egypt's gods at Passover, and then extending that claim to every plague. He called that eisegesis and a composition fallacy, arguing that the firstborn judgment strikes living designated inheritors and destabilizes Egyptian power rather than directly harming spirit beings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=8631" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:23:51</a> | Do Adam and Eve and the genealogical patriarch narratives preserve earlier Mosaic traditions, or are they later developments shaped by the Babylonian exile? | He said most scholarship pushes these materials late, but he treats that as a scholarly tendency rather than a settled conclusion. | He said mainstream biblical studies usually dates these materials to the Babylonian exile, but he does not accept that consensus as decisive. His own view places Adam and Eve and much of the Torah tradition at least in the Late Bronze Age, or at the very latest early Iron Age, while admitting that everyone is dealing with limited evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=9312" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:12</a> | Are online bachelor's or community college programs okay? | He said it depends on the goal: they can be fine for less competitive paths, but not for highly competitive fields like Egyptology. | He said online bachelor's or community college work can be fine in less competitive fields such as philosophy, and community colleges can handle some in-person lab needs. For highly competitive graduate tracks such as Egyptology, however, he warned that online preparation will not be competitive enough. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=10128" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:48:48</a> | What are the non-negotiable requirements for a church to be Christian? | He pointed to core historic doctrines such as the Trinity, salvation by grace through faith, the virgin birth, the fall, and Christ's return. | He separated orthodoxy from orthopraxis. Doctrinally, he listed historic Christian commitments such as the Trinity, incarnation, virgin birth, fall, grace through faith, crucifixion, vicarious atonement, resurrection, and return of Christ; practically, he said a church should have baptism, the Lord's Supper, Scripture reading, preaching, and worship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=10532" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:55:32</a> | What are his thoughts on Ecclesiastes 3:21? | He said the passage emphasizes that humans have no automatic advantage over beasts in death; the writer leaves much at "who knows?" | He emphasized the repeated "who knows" of the passage and read it as refusing to give humans a special automatic advantage over animals in death. Both humans and beasts live, struggle, die, and return to dust, and while revelation gives Christians hope, Ecclesiastes itself leaves the question open. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=10989" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:03:09</a> | What advice would he give a high schooler who wants to become a Bible scholar? | He warned about the practical realities of the field and urged the student to think seriously about a financially stable path alongside that goal. | He jokingly suggested accounting, then said Bible scholarship in North America is normally graduate-level work after another undergraduate degree. Almost any undergraduate major can work, but he strongly advised taking biblical Greek early, because graduate programs often require it or force students into remedial Greek without graduate credit. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=11243" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:07:23</a> | Did Egyptians use syrup in food? | Yes. He said they reduced pomegranate juice and dates into sweet syrups and also used honey. | He said Egyptians did use sweet syrups, including reduced pomegranate juice poured on food and date syrup made by reducing dates. Honey was also available, and he described these sweet preparations as desirable foods rather than rare oddities. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=11748" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:15:48</a> | What is his alternative to the documentary hypothesis? | He said he favors a supplementary hypothesis with late Bronze Age core documents later edited together with minimal connecting language. | He identified himself as a supplementary-hypothesis person. In his view, core Pentateuchal documents were written in the Late Bronze Age, collected as documents, and later strung together by an editor with limited connective material and some additions such as the death notice of Moses. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=12080" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:21:20</a> | How does clan theory work with Enoch and Abraham's ages? | He said clan theory is not limited to Genesis 5; with Enoch, the progenitor is taken away and the clan ends. | He said clan theory appears beyond Genesis 5, including Genesis 11, Abraham in Genesis 12, Exodus 6, and Chronicles. For Enoch, he said the progenitor is taken away after 365 years and the clan ends because the central figure is gone. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=12536" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:28:56</a> | How literate were Israelites during the United Monarchy? | He said probably not highly literate overall, but elites likely needed literacy, implying a baseline literate culture. | He said the United Monarchy was probably not highly literate by modern standards, but the evidence suggests a baseline literacy among elites. He cited later ostraca in which a military official is mocked for illiteracy, arguing that such mockery only works in a culture where literacy is expected for certain high-status functions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=12867" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:34:27</a> | What are Eastern Orthodox toll houses? | He described them, bluntly, as an Eastern Orthodox version of purgatorial postmortem testing. | He described toll houses as a controversial Eastern Orthodox idea in which the soul passes through a series of postmortem stations and works off sins on the way to heaven. He compared it to purgatory and noted that many Orthodox no longer accept the idea. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=13128" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:38:48</a> | Was there an indulgence system attached to Orthodox toll houses? | No. He said there was no indulgence or "get out of jail free" mechanism for toll houses. | He said the toll-house idea did not include an indulgence mechanism like the Catholic system connected to purgatory. In the toll-house model as he described it, the debt had to be paid or worked off directly, with no shortcut or release mechanism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=13223" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:40:23</a> | Will the Catholic and Orthodox churches reconcile under a new pope? | No. He said he does not see it happening and thinks the historical moment for that has passed. | He said he does not expect reunion and thinks the opportunity has passed, arguing that past reconciliation interest was tied to Eastern Orthodox need for western military support. He also argued that on several theological instincts, especially appeal to Scripture and use of the Apocrypha, Orthodoxy can be closer to Protestant positions than to Roman Catholic ones. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=13774" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:49:34</a> | Could more artifacts in Iraq's sealands provide evidence for Eden? | He said it is very unlikely because the area is underwater and buried in delta silt. | He said the Sealand problem is not just water but delta conditions: the sea and the Tigris-Euphrates silt cover the area. Although underwater archaeology can work in places such as Alexandria, he thought the Sealand environment makes finding relevant Eden evidence extremely unlikely. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=13929" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:52:09</a> | Can Jesus be called our father in a sense alongside the Father? | He said no; calling Jesus "our father" confuses Son and Father, and Isaiah 9 is better read as "father of eternity." | He warned that calling Jesus "our father" risks patripassian confusion of the Son with the Father. On Isaiah 9:6, he said the phrase is better understood literally as "father of eternity," not as making the Son the same person as the Father. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=14050" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:54:10</a> | When can we be confident that something in a biblical text is a scribal gloss? | He said glosses need textual and contextual indicators, and should not be invoked casually just to dismiss an inconvenient reading. | He said a gloss is usually explanatory material that interrupts the narrative flow, contains an insertion marker, explains a term or place, or may include a datum that does not fit. He used Numbers 13:22 on Hebron and Zoan as an example, and distinguished a gloss from a copying error. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=14409" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:00:09</a> | Is Christianity under threat from other religions? | No. He said the greatest threats are internal, including liberalism, subjective ethics, cultural pandering, and biblical ignorance. | He said other religions are not Christianity's main threat; the greatest dangers come from inside the church. He named liberalism, subjective ethics, pandering to the culture, and especially biblical ignorance, saying that not understanding the Christian faith is more dangerous than outside religions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=14774" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:06:14</a> | What is his opinion of liberation theology? | He strongly rejected it, calling it a theologized form of Marxism that makes liberation the highest imperative. | He argued that liberation theology makes liberation, often from capitalism, the highest Christian imperative and thereby theologizes Marxism. He said that in practice it tends toward political violence and gun-running because the project becomes freeing people for a communist utopian ideal rather than centering Christ. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=16220" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:30:20</a> | Does the Ipuwer Papyrus have any significance? | He said it reflects natural phenomena similar to Exodus events, but it does not record the Exodus itself. | He dated Ipuwer to the late Middle Kingdom and said it reflects natural Nile-inundation phenomena that resemble some Exodus events when viewed as hypernatural events. He was clear that it is not a record of the Exodus itself, only of similar kinds of phenomena. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=16396" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:33:16</a> | Where is the best place to buy *Ark of the Covenant*? | He said Amazon, because purchases there help keep the book stocked and visible. | He said buying through Amazon helps keep the book stocked, reviewed, and visible in the system. He contrasted that with Amazon Canada not stocking his books because of low sales, and said outside recommendations also help the book reach circles his own social media cannot reach. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=16634" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:37:14</a> | Can the book get into seminary bookstores? | He said most seminary bookstores no longer exist, though he would like *Ark of the Covenant* to be carried where possible. | He said seminary bookstores used to matter but most have closed, including ones that once influenced him. He would like *Ark of the Covenant* in such bookstores where they exist, but noted that his books are not marketed narrowly as sanitized Christian books, which complicates placement for some outlets. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H_HihkCg79Y?t=16877" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:41:17</a> | Has he heard rumors about Disney retconning the Star Wars sequel trilogy? | Yes. He said they are considering treating it as an alternate timeline or do-over from the original trilogy. | He said he had heard the rumors and described the idea as putting the sequel trilogy into an alternate timeline or effectively treating it as a do-over from the original trilogy. He framed it as Disney trying to escape unpopular creative decisions while also being stuck because some fans like the sequels. |
